item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is so similar to 2467, which it is linked to, I thought it was the same author. Really it's the same idea and same action story with the Foundation response. When I thought they were written by the same person, I was about to say, "These are redundant. You should keep this and get rid of 2467." Sorry to say, I stand by that sentiment.

Not that I particularly liked this one. It's too long for no reason. The autopsy was especially unnecessarily long and dry. We were already told these things are like sea cucumbers. No need to read a book report on sea cucumber anatomy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How did you make the final part (where it asks for the password)??? I tried everything but I could find no code for that!

item.title: Licensing Junior Staff Post - Info
item.content:encoded: Confirming that Image 1 is compliant and image 2 is taken by the author.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Biological? Check. Biomechanical elements? Check. Ocean-based? Check. All my favourite things in an SCP in one place? That's a check too! If this thing were somehow associated with the CotBG I would begin to suspect the author reached through time and space and plucked this from my brain! All the +1s

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: Big thanks for linking my scip to such a great (And one of my favorites) article, you guys are rad

item.title: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Got three links to propose here. The first two are turning the mentions of MTF Gamma-6 and Site-45 into crosslinks to their respective pages. Additionally, with SCP-2467 lurking the seas, the last thing the SCPF would want is 1264 attempting to assimilate it, or the other way around. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

SCP-1264 is located 75m below the ocean's surface1 of the South Pacific at 48°52.6′S 123°23.6′W. It is to be held in place by 8 pairs of 10kt deadweight anchors chained together and attached to SCP-1264. Mobile Task Force Gamma-6 (aka "Deep Feeders") will be tasked with performing bimonthly examinations done by remote submersibles in order to inspect the integrity of the anchors and chain. 450 naval contact mines will be positioned around, over, and under SCP-1264. The mines will be placed 20m from each other in order to sympathetically detonate in the event of a contact. Shipping lanes will be diverted in order to avoid the containment area. Since detainment, SCP-1264 has exhibited no activity but still remains buoyant.

To this:

SCP-1264 is located 75m below the ocean's surface2 of the South Pacific at 48°52.6′S 123°23.6′W. It is to be held in place by 8 pairs of 10kt deadweight anchors chained together and attached to SCP-1264. Mobile Task Force Gamma-6 (aka "Deep Feeders") will be tasked with performing bimonthly examinations done by remote submersibles in order to inspect the integrity of the anchors and chain. 450 naval contact mines will be positioned around, over, and under SCP-1264. The mines will be placed 20m from each other in order to sympathetically detonate in the event of a contact. Shipping lanes and aquatic anomalies, such as SCP-2467, will be diverted in order to avoid the containment area. Since detainment, SCP-1264 has exhibited no activity but still remains buoyant.

Changing This:

Location of Autopsy: Research Site-45

To This:

Location of Autopsy: Research Site-45

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

Footnotes
1. Below 75m is considered to be SCP-1264's crush depth based on engineering analyses.
2. Below 75m is considered to be SCP-1264's crush depth based on engineering analyses.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know… I have a hard time imagining this thing as looking like anything other than Doctor Wily's naval juggernaut in that one episode of the Mega Man cartoon, "Terror of the Seven Seas."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed this SCP and I think it is very well written, but I have one minor nitpick. I feel like the "MTF After-Action Report" does not really add anything to the article besides a sense of "Spooky scary ambiguousness". Also, accessing the report with a password is cool, but just feels like an unnecessary gimmick. Other than that, I thought it was very well-written.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The moment I've realized it is modern "Flying Dutch", I upvoted. Even completely unnecessary password gimmick doesn't ruin it for me.
Even in death, the captain still shouts his orders. And the crew obeys.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Password has always been displayed right above the button, plain as day. I still find it weird that after 3 years there are still people who can't find it. I'm still glad it's still an enjoyable gimmick.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm pretty sure the password appears after three tries? sorry if I'm wrong
Edit: I love the 'password' bit. p.s I'm in, although its a bit disappointing that it takes you to the 'incident' page, when you can already get to it without the code. :]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Loving the accessing / verification bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm very late to the party, but I enjoyed the challenge of finding the password! It was a fun little side game, that allowed me to pretend I was "hacking" into the SCP Foundation :-P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This ship's going down, all on account of the thing armoured in sunken boats…

+1. with what I just said, I don't think I need to explain why.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Foundation probably took up what they could, and uses them as needed. Maybe supplied alongside the Navy. There's no real knowing what the Foundation is and isn't capable of…or what they do/don't have.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
and 1 Juliett-class submarine (CMS-3)

But… "Juliettes" are out of service since early 1990-s.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I watched this anime called Nobunagun and in it there was one episode featuring a formerly derelict WWII-era ship that has been integrated into alien life-forms and has become something that sounded similar to this SCP. I really enjoyed that episode and actually became quite interested in this concept so this article partly won me over due to that. I personally don't really have many qualms about the writing in general (maybe some slightly better wording here and there) and this whole password-protected digital archive file doesn't really bother me. In fact, I found it to be sort of a charm to the article.

All in all, while this article wasn't mind-blowing for me, it still deserves an upBOAT! Get it? No? Okay…..

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If only I could +1 this comment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually like the password thing, it's nice. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Funny you should mention that. This idea actually came to me while listening to this song.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reading this, I kept hearing two songs in my head. This- http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=joZaJqyl2S0 and this- http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1vDWh9GNnt0

All my upvotes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Basically this. It is an innovation that will most likely not persevere in future articles and as such, I appreciated it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked the way the last collapsible was made, it was a breeze of fresh air.

item.title: You're not even alive. Not really. You're just a ship. And ships can be sunk.
(no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1264? A label given by the Foundation to give voice to their destruction.
You sail because we allow it. You will sink because we demand it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed it, very well written in my opinion. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the way the last collapsible works. Adds a nice touch of authenticity, IMO.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The classifications are combination of the two. In a 'real' Foundation, there would be a containment classification and a threat classification. What we use is attempting to show two variables on a single scale. It's a trade off between realism and not really having a need for excessive detail, that sometimes results in misunderstandings like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, it was noscript. I just allowed something on wdfiles and the button showed up. Fun!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not to mention they need to keep going down and checking on it. When the containment is itself dangerous, that's a good candidate for Keter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmmm, you are the first to complain that you can't get to the incident log. Can you see the button?

Here is the actual link if you care: http://scp-wiki.wikidot.com/incident-log-1264-d-2

Anyone else think the call sign thing is too "hokey"? [EDIT: you're probably right, it does give a bit of unwanted cheese to this article…deleted.]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't see anything where I'm supposed to to click to get to this supplementary page, likely because of noscript or something. So instead of enhancing the article, I can't read all of it.

I'm also not a fan of the 'call sign: user name' thing. That really lost its impact after the first time it was done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's not quite accurate. Something utterly uncontainable isn't going to be Keter unless it actually does bad stuff if it gets free.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ok, this is enough to convince me. In hindsight, I don't know why I had so much unwarranted apprehension on keter classification. Changed to keter.

Also added a bit more on how it was finally detained.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Usually the potential of the SCP escaping containment on its own and requiring excessive measures to contain it is grounds for classification as Keter.

I would consider the use of 450 naval mines and 160 kilotons of anchor rather excessive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Threat isn't how classification works, though. E: in my headcanon

Safe: Lock it in a box
Euclid: Lock it in a box and watch it for funny tricks
Keter: Oh my god, have a thousand automated turrets around this bloody box, which better have another box around it because he's gonna break out of the first.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Concurring with Mackenzie. Rest of article is really good, but the password is just too much. Downvoted, will be changed if removed.
Cancelling vote, until I decide one way or the other.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Concurring with wishun about it being reasonable to have occasional times where the article isn't immediately printable, but rather a digital archive. Another good example, presently rated at +276, is Mack's own SCP-895.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I respectfully disagree with Raven and balance that vote with my own.

The mainlist is ostensibly a digital archive, in-universe. As evidenced by the 001 over page, among others. I wouldn't want to see this become common, but as a one-off it's a novel gimmick that fits the setting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Many keters (682 for instance), don't pose much of a threat to civilization as a whole. We only know of the ships that saw it and lived to tell the tale. This thing has a pretty huge body count, and actively seems to hunt down and kill people. And it's not just doing it with stealth or preying on defenseless civilians (Well, it is, but it's powerful enough to go toe-to-toe with almost anything we have).

That's not really well conveyed. I would make that a bit more explicit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is incredibly obnoxious and I am downvoting until such time that it's removed. I maintain that SCP articles should be written in such a way as to make them printable. This is not printable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I will try to answer both of these questions. As for the classification, I was kind of torn on whether to actually classify as keter. Though it murders lots of people, it does not pose a threat to civilization itself and can't really cause that much destruction if it got out…with the exception of destroying the occasional fishing trawler every now and again. My point is, this thing really doesn't have a high frequency of incidents. It's been cruising the ocean for sixty years or so…and has only been seen by a number of ships somewhere in the double digits. That is my perspective, but I am totally not against changing the classification if I am wrong here.

The second part…I was trying to insinuate in the epilogue that it was eventually outrunned and outgunned. Once dead in the water, they hauled it out to the most remote spot in the ocean and strapped it down. If this needs to be elaborated on, I can certainly do that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had wondered this myself. It blew away half of the team sent to contain it in one salvo; did we just send more ships and anchor the fucker down? Or did we cripple it during that fight?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry, how did we get this into containment? I may have missed something, but last we saw this in the article it was still roaming free (incidentally, before the Foundation caught this, and maybe even afterwards, this is totally Keter).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh my friend, it will be now. Watch every newbie article for the next two weeks have a password on it. I can hear it, the relentless clicking of the CTRL and V keys now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, I'm in. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked the password bit. Surprised that hasn't been used on other SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
450 naval contact mines will be positioned around, over, and under SCP-1264.

Either that or 450 naval mimes. Y'know, whatever works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fair enough, let me keep chipping away at the incident log in the meantime. Maybe I can twist it around. (EDIT: added a spritzing of conspiracy)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Saying that they otherwise appear to be human (if that is indeed the case) would help a lot.

It does reveal things that I don't mention in the article (SCP-1246-1 is pretty deceptive and SCP-1246 can self-repair).

This isn't especially interesting, though. I mean, it adds character, but it isn't any sort of big reveal that would need some kind of protection. It isn't really disturbing, horrifying, no religious or cultural implications, no real consequences for the information getting out that wouldn't also be felt if the main SCP description itself was leaked… etc. Why would the Foundation hide this information?

Actually, there's no reason not to put that in main article. In fact, it's kind of relevant information that seriously belongs in the main description, now that you mention it. Not necessarily the interview, but the fact that it can self-repair and is deceptive.

NP. Hope it helps :)

edit: also -

(EDIT: see if that is any better.)

Works pretty perfectly for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Per your's and Moose's advice, I think I found a tasteful way to add the password into the actual article without breaking immersion. Better? Doesn't help?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was leaving all the detail for SCP-1246-A in the autopsy, hoping that it would satisfy those clarity issues. I guess I could put in some quick details about SCP-1246-A in the actual description, but like you said, I need to put it and not overkill the article with detail. I could bump down the clearance to level 3, I see no problem with that. It does reveal things that I don't mention in the article (SCP-1246-1 is pretty deceptive and SCP-1246 can self-repair). Let me try and think of a way to subtly put that password in the page…currently nothing comes to mind, suggestions? (EDIT: see if that is any better.)

Echo and I tried to spice up the incident log with that addendum…but I guess it is still falling short, hmmm. I need to lean back in my chair and think. (EDIT: check the incident log with the changes I made, am I headed in the right direction?)

Thanks for the feedback, moose.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I got through the whole article without ever knowing what 1264-A looks like. Am I assuming that they look like humans, except for X, Y, and Z? Also, you're bordering on the edge of excess detail, which is especially a problem when clarity is also an issue.

The incident log is kind of 'meh', and there's no reason for it to be Level 4. Maybe Level 2. Level 3 if I missed some implication of the log. Level 4 is usually "SUPER DUPER IMPORTANT STUFF OMG" and a lot of that is even Level 3, and all this was was a log of some random incident with a bit of dialogue that doesn't reveal much. (Correct me if I missed something.)

However, the overall idea is kind of neat, and I like some of that excess detail. Also, the trick with the password popup is entertaining, as I and others mentioned before. (Not everybody is gonna like it, but you can't please everybody. Also, FWIW, I don't see any problem with having the password on the page itself, and having some nonsense about how your user has been approved, which you basically already have anyway. We can pretend that in-universe it's ~computer magic~)

I think you could have more interesting elements in this piece, as well. The incident log stands out as a missed opportunity.

So on balance, +1, but I encourage you to take a second look at some of those issues.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1246 is the actual ship
SCP-1246-A is the crew
SCP-1246-1 is the "captain" or the "controller"

As I was writing this out, I found that separating the other two was more fluid than referring them as "SCP-1246's crew" or "SCP-1246's captain".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You beat me to it. Thanks again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I fixed it. Didn't consider that changing the URL of the log page would affect this page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To be specific, the former page does not exist.

http://scp-wiki.wikidot.com/incident-log-1264-d-2 would also work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Uh, your button leads to http://scp-wiki.wikidot.com/incident-log:1264-d-2 instead of http://www.scp-wiki.net/incident-log-1264-d-2

Might wanna fix that

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ok, now it looks better. But what is exactly The real SCP still avoids me. I know that there is SCP-A, but i sdon't know its shape. I know there is SCP-1, but you made it with an uknown shape on porpuse. Now, what's exactly this SCP? Ok, you started abotu a lot of mixed WWII-era ships and aircrafts, but what about the Biological tissue? You started telling me metal, and from suddenly it became organic.
